text.fullText: Public Comments Submi ed to the U.S. Environmental Protec on Agency re: Upda ng the Water Quality Cer ﬁca on Regula ons Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Submi ed by: Commi ee for a Construc ve Tomorrow (CFACT) 1717 Pennsylvania Ave. N.W. Washington, D.C. 20006 February 2, 2026 The Commi ee for a Construc ve Tomorrow (CFACT), a 501 (c) (3) nonproﬁt organiza on based in Washington, D.C., is pleased to comment on EPA’s proposed rule to update and clarify several substan ve and procedural requirements for water quality cer ﬁca on under Sec on 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). We support the goal of EPA’s proposed rule, which is to make the water quality cer ﬁca on process consistent with the CWA’s legal framework. The proposed rule addresses problems that have surfaced in the implementa on of Sec on 401’s gran ng of direct authority to States (and Tribes that have been approved for “treatment as a State” status) to review for compliance with appropriate Federal, State, and Tribal water quality requirements any discharges into waters of the United States that may result from a proposed ac vity that that requires a Federal license or permit. Unfortunately, Sec on 401’s legi mate delega on of authority to States and authorized Tribes to review proposed ac vi es requiring a Federal license or permit is being undermined by a rule issued by the previous administra on that allows States and Tribes to block projects for reasons that have nothing to do with discharges from point sources into waters of the United States. Speciﬁcally, the Biden administra on’s 2023 “Clean Water Act Sec on 401 Water Quality Cer ﬁca on Improvement Rule” expanded the scope far beyond what is consistent with the CWA’s focus on discharges, point sources, and navigable waters. Under the 2023 rule, cer fying en es can deny a permit for reasons completely extraneous to the requirements laid out in sec on 401. The inclusion in the 2023 of the phrase “ac vity as a whole” broadened the scope of sec on 401 reviews beyond any reasonable reading of what the CWA’s sec on 401 set out to do. This language in the 2023 rule, EPA now correctly notes, “provides States with sweeping authority to decide the fate of na onally important infrastructure projects, such as natural-gas pipelines and hydropower dams, based on poten ally specula ve water quality impacts not linked to a point source discharge into waters of the United States.” 1 The dele on of that phrase in the current proposal restores the historical statutory limita ons to sec on 401 reviews. This will prevent States from using Sec on 401 to address nonpoint source impacts, impacts to purely state waters that are not considered waters of the United States, and non-water quality policy concerns, such as land use, endangered species, and climate change. Further interference by States in the sec on 401 approval process will be sharply reduced by the proposed rule’s crea on of a uniform, na onal standard for what a permit applicant must submit in a “request for cer ﬁca on” to establish, with certainty, the start of the one-year statutory period for States to act on a water-quality cer ﬁca on request. The proposed rule also bars States from imposing their own “completeness” criteria that control when the one- year clock starts. However, in allowing States to seek addi onal informa on from applicants, EPA needs to ensure that these requests do not become open-ended exercises, designed to bring about delays. This part of the rule could be strengthened by imposing strict limits on such requests, so that these requests and responses, cumula vely, are concluded within 30 days. Also concerning delays, the proposed rule reiterates that the failure of a State to act on a water- quality cer ﬁca on request within one year results in a waiver, while imposing several restric ons on States’ ability to extend the one-year cer ﬁca on deadline. Automa c extensions and force majeure would be eliminated. Extensions would require the approval of the Federal agency and would only be allowed for speciﬁed reasons, and State cer fying agencies would be barred from orchestra ng withdrawal and resubmission of requests to extend the one-year “reasonable period of me” cap – “a maneuver that has long been cri cized by applicants as gamesmanship that further facilitates the injec on of non-water quality policy priori es into the cer ﬁca on process.” 2 The proposed rule correctly requires States to explain how each condi on of a cer ﬁca on relates to a CWA eﬄuent limita on and would mandate any water-quality cer ﬁca on denial to cite the speciﬁc water-quality requirement at issue. With this s pula on, the proposed rule may deter certain State eﬀorts to prolong the water-quality cer ﬁca on process by denying applica ons without prejudice. 1 Brooks M. Smith, Andrea W. Wortzel, Shawn J. Zovod, Charles Sensiba, Josh Kaplowitz, Stephanie M. Collins, “EPA’s Proposed CWA Rule Narrows Scope of State Reviews, Reins in Timelines,” Troutman Pepper Locks, Jan. 20, 2026 h ps://www.troutman.com/insights/epas-proposed-cwa-401-rule-narrows-scope-of-state-reviews-reins-in- melines/ 2 Ibid. Further circumscribing States’ ability to act outside the restric ons set down in Sec on 401, the proposed rule would allow States to modify a previously granted water-quality cer ﬁca on only when the Federal permi ng agency and the applicant agree to the modiﬁca on, including the content of that modiﬁca on. We support the proposed rule’s provision streamlining the process by which other States may object to a discharge that “may aﬀect” their water quality under CWA Sec on 401 (a) (2), including establishing a 60-day deadline for the other state to ﬁle an objec on and a 90-day deadline for the permi ng agency to act on a valid State objec on. “In aggregate,” as the law ﬁrm Troutman Pepper Locke has noted, “the proposed amendments would create a WQC process that is more predictable, provides long-term certainty for WQC holders, and signiﬁcantly constrains States’ ability to bootstrap other policy concerns onto their Sec on 401 authority.” 3 Indeed, with its proposed rule, EPA has pledged to “restore Sec on 401 to its proper statutory purpose.” 4 Though the proposal is certain to face legal challenges by par es that stand to beneﬁt from delaying or blocking projects essen al to upgrading the na on’s infrastructure, EPA, by hewing to Sec on 401’s statutory requirements, has put itself in a posi on to prevail in court. Thank you very much. Sincerely, Bonner Russell Cohen, Ph. D. Senior Policy Analyst Commi ee for a Construc ve Tomorrow. 3 Ibid. 4 “EPA Proposes Overhaul of Water Permit Sign—Oﬀ Rules,” ReedSmith, Jan. 23, 2026 h ps://www.reedsmith.com/our-insights/blogs/viewpoints/102mdwv/epa-proposes-overhaul-of-water-permit- sign-oﬀ-rules/
